serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed black tea, approximately 240 ml, served hot without milk or sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain brewed black tea, unsweetened, prepared with water. Values are per 100 g of brewed beverage. This is the closest exact match for plain brewed black tea without milk or sugar.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of beverages: plain brewed black tea (unsweetened) — effectively zero GI; convention value assigned
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain unsweetened black tea contains negligible carbohydrates (0.3 g per 100 g) and has no measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature confirm that plain tea does not raise blood glucose. Per Project Veritas convention for zero-GI beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving is 0.0 due to negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain unsweetened black tea is appropriate for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); epidemiological evidence also suggests regular tea consumption may modestly support vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and no glycemic impact when consumed without sugar or milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain unsweetened black tea is safe for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains only 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g, produces no measurable glycemic response, and polyphenols in black tea may support insulin sensitivity; however, additions of sugar or condensed milk must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of flavonoid antioxidants (theaflavins, thearubigins)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain black tea is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its zero fat and zero cholesterol content; regular consumption of black tea has been associated in peer-reviewed literature with modest improvements in LDL cholesterol and endothelial function due to its flavonoid content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black tea (红茶, Hóng chá) is a fully oxidised tea made from the leaves of Camellia sinensis. The Fengmei (凤眉) variety served at CHICHA San Chen in Singapore is a heavily roasted Chinese black tea known for its mellow, sweet flavour profile with a crisp and clean finish. Unlike green or oolong teas, black tea undergoes complete oxidation during processing, which develops its characteristic dark colour, robust body, and complex flavour compounds including theaflavins and thearubigins. When brewed plain without milk or sugar, black tea is essentially a zero-calorie, zero-fat beverage containing only trace carbohydrates (approximately 0.3 g per 100 g) and negligible sodium. It is naturally caffeine-containing, with approximately 20–45 mg caffeine per 240 ml cup depending on steeping time and leaf grade. Nutritionally, plain black tea contributes minimal macronutrients but provides potassium (37 mg per 100 g), small amounts of folate, and bioactive polyphenols. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and individuals following halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or full-fat milk.
common_user_queries: Is black tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in black tea?
Is black tea suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain brewed black tea (NDB No. 14355 / FDC ID 171917), which is an exact match for unsweetened brewed black tea. GI is assigned by convention (value 1) consistent with peer-reviewed evidence that plain tea has no measurable glycemic response. Multilingual names are verified standard translations.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 g (trace); this is the USDA-reported value for brewed black tea and is not zero. 2. protein_g is naturally zero — plain brewed black tea contains no protein by nature of the beverage. 3. fat_g is naturally zero — plain brewed black tea contains no fat by nature of the beverage. 4. sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for unsweetened brewed black tea. 5. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for brewed black tea liquid. 6. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Project Veritas convention for zero-GI beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 since carbohydrate content is negligible. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0 as confirmed by USDA source for plain brewed black tea. 8. The input food_name 'Black Tea凤眉红茶' has been normalised to canonical English 'Black Tea'; the Chinese variety name 凤眉红茶 (Fengmei Black Tea) is preserved in aliases. 9. The input cuisine_tags included 'Milk Tea' which is not applicable to this plain black tea record; this tag has been disregarded as it likely reflects a menu category rather than the food itself. 10. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 and SNOMED CT 227313005 are used as best available matches; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for tea infusion. Reviewer should verify FoodEx2 code against the official EFSA FoodEx2 browser. 11. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with 0.0 or actual values from the USDA source where data is available; no -1 values remain in this record as the USDA SR Legacy entry provides comprehensive data for brewed black tea.
